---
title: "Linear transforms as a Lie group"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
This is the substrate page of the registration unit: the **linear transforms** —
rigid, similarity, affine — that the rigid/affine rungs of the
[transform ladder](foundations.qmd#sec-ladder) optimise over. One idea organises
everything:

- A linear transform is a **homogeneous matrix** $\mathbf{A}$, composed by matrix
  multiply and inverted by matrix inverse. You can read a transform two ways: as a
  **geometric factorisation** $\mathbf{A}=T\,R\,S\,E$ (translate · rotate · scale ·
  shear), good for *reading off and constraining* meaning, or as the
  **exponential of a Lie-algebra element**, good for *optimising*.
- **An optimiser should move on the tangent space at the identity, not on the group
  itself.** The Lie chart $\exp:\mathfrak g\to G$ turns "find a rotation" — a
  constrained problem on a curved surface of orthogonal matrices — into "find a
  vector" — an unconstrained problem in flat space. `rigid_exp` (Rodrigues) and
  `affine_exp` (`matrix_exp`) are that chart.
- Transforms form a **Riemannian manifold**, so averaging and interpolating them
  (template construction, motion summary) must go through `log`/`exp`, never an
  entrywise mean.

The single new skill: read a transform as a point on a curved group, and an
*update* to it as a vector in flat tangent space.
:::

This unit is written for a reader fluent in **linear algebra** — matrices, least
squares, eigendecomposition, the chain rule — who has read the geometry unit
(grids and meshes as discrete stand-ins for smooth manifolds) but has had **no
formal training in image registration, Lie theory, or numerical optimisation**.
The previous page, [Foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-ladder), set up the governing
idea — *a registration is a map between two geometries, found by minimising a
mismatch energy over a parametrised family of transforms* — and laid out the
transform ladder. This page owns the bottom two rungs: the **linear** transforms
and the algebra that makes them optimisable, averageable, and composable. It is the
sibling of the geometry unit's
[volumes page](../geometry/volumes.qmd) (same house style).

## A motivating question {#sec-motivation}

Hold one concrete question for the whole page. A subject lies in the scanner for a
ten-minute fMRI run; over those ten minutes their head drifts and nods. Each volume
in the time series is the same head in a slightly different **pose**, and before we
can compare voxel $i$ across volumes we must *realign* them — undo the motion. The
classic question: **what six numbers describe a rigid head motion?**

"Rigid" because a head does not stretch or shear between two seconds — it only
*rotates* and *translates*. In 3-D that is three rotation angles and three
translations: six numbers, the six **degrees of freedom** of a rigid-body motion.
The realignment problem is then: for each volume, find the six numbers that best
bring it back onto a reference. That immediately raises three sub-questions this
page answers in turn:

1. How do we *represent* a rigid (or affine) transform so we can apply it to every
   voxel and compose a chain of them? (Homogeneous matrices, @sec-homogeneous.)
2. How do we *parametrise* it with exactly six (or twelve) numbers so an optimiser
   can search — and so that every point in the search space is a valid transform?
   (The Lie exponential chart, @sec-lie-exp; the geometric factorisation,
   @sec-decomposition.)
3. Once we have a transform per volume, how do we *summarise* the motion — its mean
   pose, a halfway pose — without leaving the space of valid transforms?
   (The transform manifold, @sec-transform-manifold.)

The same machinery aligns a subject's T1 to MNI (affine, twelve numbers), or builds
a study template from a cohort of transforms. Rigid head motion is just the
smallest instance.

## The homogeneous matrix {#sec-homogeneous}

A linear map $x\mapsto Mx$ fixes the origin: it can rotate, scale, and shear, but it
cannot *move* anything that sits at $0$. A head motion includes a translation, so a
bare $N\times N$ matrix is not enough. The obvious fix is to carry a separate
translation vector $t$ and write $x\mapsto Mx+t$ — and that works, but it is clumsy:
composing two such maps, $(M_2,t_2)\circ(M_1,t_1)$, is *not* a single
matrix multiply (you get $M_2M_1$ for the linear part but $M_2t_1+t_2$ for the
translation), so a chain of transforms is a chain of special-case bookkeeping.

The cure is one idea: **lift the coordinate one dimension**. Append a $1$ to every
point, $x\mapsto(x,1)$, and stack the linear block and the translation into one
$(N{+}1)\times(N{+}1)$ matrix with a fixed bottom row:

$$
\mathbf{A}=\begin{pmatrix} M & t \\ \mathbf 0^\top & 1 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\mathbf{A}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
=\begin{pmatrix} Mx+t \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}.
$$ {#eq-homogeneous}

Now the affine map $Mx+t$ *is* a matrix–vector product, and — the payoff —
**composition is plain matrix multiplication**: $\mathbf{A}_2\mathbf{A}_1$ applies
$\mathbf{A}_1$ first, then $\mathbf{A}_2$, with the translations handled
automatically by the extra row. The extra dimension is the price; uniform algebra is
what you buy. This is the canonical $\mathbf A$ of the
[notation table](foundations.qmd#sec-spaces): an $(N{+}1)\times(N{+}1)$ homogeneous
matrix, affine block $\mathbf A_{:N,:N}$, translation $\mathbf A_{:N,N}$.

`nitrix` stores affines compactly as the **rectangular** $(N,N{+}1)$ form — the top
$N$ rows, since the bottom row $(\mathbf 0^\top,1)$ is implied — and squares them up
only when it needs the full matrix algebra.

- **`make_square_affine(mat)`** — `(..., N, N+1)` → `(..., N+1, N+1)`, appending the
  fixed row $(\mathbf 0^\top,1)$ (a no-op if already square). **DIFF-JAX**: pure
  array construction, batched over leading dimensions.
- **`compose_affine(mats)`** — composes a *sequence* of affines **left-to-right**:
  `compose_affine((A, B, C))` returns $\mathbf A\mathbf B\mathbf C$, which applies
  **C first, then B, then A** (the last listed is applied first, exactly as nested
  application $\mathbf A(\mathbf B(\mathbf C x))$ reads). It squares each operand,
  multiplies, and drops the implied row back off. **DIFF-JAX**. (Note the signature
  takes one `Sequence`, not varargs.)
- **`invert_affine(mat)`** — the multiplicative inverse $\mathbf A^{-1}$, returned in
  the same rectangular form. It squares the matrix, calls the inverse, and slices the
  top rows back.

The composition order is worth dwelling on because it is the source of most affine
bugs. A registration pipeline is "rigid, *then* affine, *then* deformable" — the
order you apply stages to the moving image. As matrices that reads **right-to-left**
under the convention of @eq-homogeneous (the rightmost matrix touches the coordinate
first), so the pipeline matrix is $\mathbf A_{\text{def}}\mathbf A_{\text{aff}}\mathbf
A_{\text{rig}}$ and the `compose_affine` argument list is
`(A_def, A_aff, A_rig)` — *reverse* of the application order in prose. Hold that
straight and affine plumbing stops biting.

### Why `safe_*` and analytic determinants {#sec-safe}

`invert_affine` does not call `jnp.linalg.inv` directly; it calls
`linalg._solver.safe_inv`. The reason is a hardware reality, not a numerical one.
On certain CUDA/JAX stacks the **cuSolver handle pool is broken** — an ABI mismatch
that makes every dense factorisation (`inv`, `solve`, `cholesky`, `eigh`, `qr`,
`svd`) fail at runtime with `gpusolverDnCreate failed`, while matmul, conv, and
triangular solves keep working. The `safe_*` wrappers (`safe_inv`,
`safe_cholesky`, `safe_cho_solve`) probe a $2\times2$ call once, cache the verdict,
and **adaptively latch** to the CPU if a real call fails at runtime, moving the
result back to the originating device. They are the cuSolver-robust seam the geometry
unit's [solver page](../geometry/solvers.qmd) introduces, reused here.

Where it can, `affine.py` sidesteps the factorisation entirely by computing
**analytically**: the $2\times2$ and $3\times3$ determinants (`_det2x2`, `_det3x3`)
are cofactor expansions, pure matmul-free arithmetic, GPU-native on any stack. The
rule the geometry unit states applies here too: the differentiable hot loop of an
optimiser must stay on closed-form, cuSolver-free paths; the factorisation-bearing
ops (`invert_affine`, `fit_affine`, the Karcher mean of @sec-transform-manifold) are
either rare or offline.

## Reading a transform: the geometric factorisation {#sec-decomposition}

The homogeneous matrix is the right object to *apply*, but its sixteen entries are
opaque: you cannot look at a $4\times4$ matrix and say "this rotates 12° and scales
the $z$-axis by 1.1." Yet those are exactly the quantities a practitioner wants — to
*read off* ("did the fit pick up an implausible 30% scale?") and to *constrain*
("force this fit to be rigid"). For that we need a parametrisation in which the
geometric factors are individually named.

Any linear map decomposes — uniquely, up to sign conventions — into four geometric
operations applied in a fixed order. `nitrix` uses **$\mathbf A = T\,R\,S\,E$**:

$$
\mathbf A \;=\; \underbrace{T}_{\text{translate}}\;
\underbrace{R}_{\text{rotate}}\;
\underbrace{S}_{\text{scale}}\;
\underbrace{E}_{\text{shear}},
$$ {#eq-trse}

read right-to-left as the order applied to a point: **shear** $E$ first (a
unit-diagonal upper-triangular matrix that slides one axis along another), then
anisotropic **scale** $S$ (a diagonal matrix, one factor per axis), then **rotate**
$R$ (an orthogonal matrix, $\det=+1$), then **translate** $T$. @fig-transforms-trse
builds exactly this product one factor at a time on a unit square.

```{python}
#| label: fig-transforms-trse
#| fig-cap: "The geometric factorisation $\\mathbf A=T\\,R\\,S\\,E$ applied to a unit square, one factor at a time (the faint grey square is the identity reference in every panel). Reading right-to-left in the product, the shear $E$ slides the top edge sideways; the anisotropic scale $S=\\mathrm{diag}(1.6,0.7)$ stretches $x$ and squashes $y$; the rotation $R$ (30°) turns the whole figure; the translation $T$ shifts it bodily. This is the order `params_to_affine_matrix` composes, and the factors are exactly what `affine_matrix_to_params` reads back off a matrix."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A unit square (closed polygon), columns are points.
sq = np.array([[0, 1, 1, 0, 0],
               [0, 0, 1, 1, 0]], dtype=float)

def hom(M2, t=(0.0, 0.0)):
    H = np.eye(3)
    H[:2, :2] = M2
    H[:2, 2] = t
    return H

theta = np.radians(30.0)
R = np.array([[np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta)],
              [np.sin(theta),  np.cos(theta)]])
S = np.diag([1.6, 0.7])                 # anisotropic scale
E = np.array([[1.0, 0.5], [0.0, 1.0]])  # shear (strict-upper entry s=0.5)
t = np.array([1.2, 0.4])                # translation

stages = [
    ("identity",         np.eye(3),          '0.6'),
    ("E  shear",         hom(E),             '#4477aa'),
    ("S·E  + scale",     hom(S @ E),         '#66ccee'),
    ("R·S·E  + rotate",  hom(R @ S @ E),     '#ee6677'),
    ("T·R·S·E  + shift", hom(R @ S @ E, t),  '#228833'),
]

def apply(H, pts):
    p = np.vstack([pts, np.ones((1, pts.shape[1]))])
    return (H @ p)[:2]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 5, figsize=(15, 3.2), sharex=True, sharey=True)
for ax, (name, H, col) in zip(axes, stages):
    ax.plot(sq[0], sq[1], color='0.85', lw=1.0)
    out = apply(H, sq)
    ax.fill(out[0], out[1], color=col, alpha=0.25)
    ax.plot(out[0], out[1], color=col, lw=2.0)
    ax.set_title(name, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_aspect('equal')
    ax.axhline(0, color='0.9', lw=0.5, zorder=0)
    ax.axvline(0, color='0.9', lw=0.5, zorder=0)
    ax.set_xlim(-0.8, 3.0)
    ax.set_ylim(-0.8, 2.6)

fig.suptitle("$\\mathbf{A}=T\\,R\\,S\\,E$ built one factor at a time (rightmost applied first)",
             fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.94])
plt.show()
```

### Parameters in, matrix out — and back {#sec-params}

**`params_to_affine_matrix(par, *, ndim=3, deg=True, shift_scale=False)`** assembles
@eq-trse from a flat parameter vector. The parameter order along the last axis is
**translation, rotation, scale, shear**, with segment lengths $(d,\ d(d{-}1)/2,\ d,\
d(d{-}1)/2)$ — so in 3-D that is $(3,3,3,3)=12$ numbers, in 2-D $(2,1,2,1)=6$.
Crucially, **missing trailing parameters default to identity**: scale defaults to
$1$, everything else to $0$. So a length-6 prefix in 3-D (three translations, three
angles) gives a **rigid** transform — rotation and translation only, unit scale, no
shear — without your having to spell out the trailing identity factors. The function
takes an explicit `ndim` precisely because a 6-vector is ambiguous: it could be a
rigid 3-D transform or a full 2-D one. `shift_scale=True` adds $1$ to the scale
segment so that the all-zeros vector is the identity — the convention you want when
the parameters are a *network-predicted perturbation* around identity. **DIFF-JAX**,
batched.

**`affine_matrix_to_params(mat, *, deg=True)`** is the inverse: it reads the four
geometric factors back off a matrix (rectangular $(d,d{+}1)$ or square $(d{+}1,d{+}1)$
— `ndim` is inferred from the shape). The mechanism is an **RQ-style decomposition
via Cholesky**. The linear block $M$ factors as $R\,S\,E$ with $R$ orthogonal and
$S\,E$ upper-triangular; the Gram matrix $M^\top M = (SE)^\top R^\top R (SE) =
(SE)^\top(SE)$ drops the rotation, so its Cholesky factor *is* the lower-triangular
$(SE)^\top$. The diagonal of that factor gives the **scales**, the off-diagonal
(after dividing out the scales) the **shears**, and $R = M(SE)^{-1}$ recovers the
rotation, which `rotation_matrix_to_angles` turns into angles. A determinant-sign fix
on the first scale lets a reflection ($\det<0$) round-trip. **DIFF-JAX**, but it
calls `safe_cholesky` and `safe_inv`, so it rides the cuSolver-robust seam — fine for
construction, not for a hot loop.

These two are exact inverses (round-tripping to machine precision in the
non-degenerate regime), which is what makes the factorisation *trustworthy* for
reading off meaning: a fitted affine's scales are the diagonal of a Cholesky factor,
not a vague impression.

### Rotations and Euler angles {#sec-euler}

The rotation factor $R$ has its own chart, **`angles_to_rotation_matrix`** /
**`rotation_matrix_to_angles`**, with a dimension-specific convention:

- **2-D** is a single planar angle $\theta$, $(\cos,-\sin;\sin,\cos)$ — and its
  inverse is one `atan2`.
- **3-D** is **right-handed intrinsic** $R = R_x R_y R_z$ (rotate about $x$, then
  $y$, then $z$), built from the nine entries in @eq-trse's $R$ block. Angles are in
  **degrees by default** (`deg=False` for radians).

Three Euler angles are a beautiful and cursed chart. Beautiful because they are the
fewest numbers (three) that name a 3-D rotation in human terms; cursed because the
map *degenerates* when the middle rotation reaches $\pm90°$ — the first and third
axes line up and you lose a degree of freedom. This is **gimbal lock**, and naïvely
inverting $R\to$ angles divides by $\cos(\text{ang}_1)=0$ there.
`rotation_matrix_to_angles` handles it with an explicit branch: when
$|\text{ang}_1|=90°$ (detected as $\big||\text{ang}_1|-\tfrac\pi2\big|<10^{-6}$) it
fixes $\text{ang}_0=0$ and solves the remaining angle from a different matrix entry,
and a guarded `_divide_no_nan` keeps the generic branch finite. The lesson — *Euler
angles are a chart with a coordinate singularity* — is exactly why the optimiser does
**not** use them, and why @sec-lie-exp exists.

The practitioner's payoff of this whole section is **constraint by construction**. A
*rigid* fit is "unit scale, zero shear" — so you keep only the translation+rotation
prefix of the parameter vector and let `params_to_affine_matrix` default the rest. A
*similarity* (7-DOF) fit ties the three scales to a single isotropic factor. You
never have to project a free matrix back onto the rigid manifold; you withhold the
unwanted factors from the parameter vector and let the defaults fill them in.

## The Lie exponential chart {#sec-lie-exp}

Here is the central idea of the page, and the reason registration borrows from Lie
theory at all. We want to *optimise* over rotations (rigid) or invertible maps
(affine). Both are **curved, constrained sets**: a rotation matrix must satisfy
$R^\top R = I$ and $\det R = +1$ (a six-equation constraint on nine numbers); an
orientation-preserving invertible matrix must have $\det>0$. If an optimiser nudges
the nine entries of $R$ freely, it walks *off* the set of rotations — the result is no
longer orthogonal, and "the nearest rotation" is a re-projection it must keep
redoing. Euler angles avoid that but reintroduce gimbal lock (@sec-euler). We want a
chart with **no constraints and no singularity at the working point**.

The obvious starting move: a rotation near the identity is "identity plus a little
skew." Recall that for a curve of rotations $R(s)$ with $R(0)=I$, the derivative
$\dot R(0)$ is **skew-symmetric** ($\Omega^\top=-\Omega$) — that is the linearisation
of the constraint $R^\top R=I$. So the **tangent space at the identity** is the space
of skew-symmetric matrices, which in 3-D is parametrised by an axis-angle vector
$\omega\in\mathbb R^3$ through

$$
[\omega]_\times=\begin{pmatrix}0&-\omega_z&\omega_y\\\omega_z&0&-\omega_x\\
-\omega_y&\omega_x&0\end{pmatrix},
$$ {#eq-skew}

the skew-symmetric matrix of $\omega$ (so that $[\omega]_\times v=\omega\times v$).
This space — skew-symmetric matrices in 3-D, plus a free translation — is the **Lie
algebra** $\mathfrak{se}(d)$, the flat tangent space at the identity of the rigid
group $SE(d)$. It is *unconstrained*: any $\omega$ is a valid element, any small
nudge stays in the algebra. The optimiser lives here.

The bridge from the flat algebra back to the curved group is the **exponential map**.
For rotations it has a closed form — **Rodrigues' formula** — which you can almost
derive by hand. The matrix exponential is $\exp(\Omega)=\sum_k \Omega^k/k!$; for a
skew-symmetric $\Omega=[\omega]_\times$ with angle $\theta=\|\omega\|$, the powers
collapse because $[\omega]_\times^3=-\theta^2[\omega]_\times$, and the series folds
into sines and cosines:

$$
R=\exp([\omega]_\times)=I+\frac{\sin\theta}{\theta}[\omega]_\times
+\frac{1-\cos\theta}{\theta^2}[\omega]_\times^2.
$$ {#eq-rodrigues}

Read it as: rotate about the axis $\omega/\theta$ by the angle $\theta=\|\omega\|$.
The $\sin\theta/\theta$ term is the first-order turn, the
$(1-\cos\theta)/\theta^2$ term the second-order curvature correction — exactly the
two terms you would get truncating the series and grouping. @fig-transforms-rodrigues
shows $\exp$ of a single skew generator turning a fan of vectors rigidly as the angle
grows.

```{python}
#| label: fig-transforms-rodrigues
#| fig-cap: "Rodrigues in action. A fan of unit vectors is rotated by $R=\\exp([\\omega]_\\times)$ for $\\omega=(0,0,\\theta)$ — a generator about the $z$-axis — at six increasing angles from $0$ to $90°$. Every blade turns by the same angle and keeps unit length: $\\exp$ of a skew-symmetric generator is always a proper rotation, with no constraint to enforce and no re-orthogonalisation. This is the map `rigid_exp` applies in closed form, and the reason an optimiser can move freely on the flat axis-angle vector $\\omega$."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def skew(w):
    x, y, z = w
    return np.array([[0, -z, y], [z, 0, -x], [-y, x, 0]])

def rodrigues(w):
    th = np.linalg.norm(w)
    K = skew(w)
    if th < 1e-12:
        return np.eye(3) + K
    return (np.eye(3)
            + (np.sin(th) / th) * K
            + ((1 - np.cos(th)) / th**2) * (K @ K))

phis = np.linspace(-50, 50, 9) * np.pi / 180.0          # the fan blades
fan = np.stack([np.cos(phis), np.sin(phis), np.zeros_like(phis)], axis=1)
angles = np.linspace(0, np.pi / 2, 6)                   # increasing rotation

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.2, 6.2))
cmap = plt.cm.viridis
for j, th in enumerate(angles):
    R = rodrigues(np.array([0.0, 0.0, th]))
    rot = fan @ R.T
    col = cmap(j / (len(angles) - 1))
    for k in range(fan.shape[0]):
        ax.plot([0, rot[k, 0]], [0, rot[k, 1]], color=col, lw=1.4, alpha=0.9)
    ax.plot(rot[:, 0], rot[:, 1], 'o', color=col, ms=3,
            label=f'$\\theta={np.degrees(th):.0f}^\\circ$')

ax.set_aspect('equal')
ax.set_xlim(-0.4, 1.15)
ax.set_ylim(-0.85, 1.15)
ax.axhline(0, color='0.8', lw=0.6, zorder=0)
ax.axvline(0, color='0.8', lw=0.6, zorder=0)
ax.set_xlabel('$x$')
ax.set_ylabel('$y$')
ax.set_title("Rodrigues: $R=\\exp([\\omega]_\\times)$ rotates a fan rigidly")
ax.legend(loc='lower left', fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### `rigid_exp` / `rigid_log` and the small-angle guard {#sec-rigid-exp}

**`rigid_exp(params, *, ndim)`** is @eq-rodrigues plus a direct translation, packaged
as a homogeneous matrix. The parameter layout is **rotation first, translation last**:
3-D is $(\omega_x,\omega_y,\omega_z,t_x,t_y,t_z)$ (6); 2-D is $(\theta,t_x,t_y)$ (3).
The rotation block is the closed-form $\exp$ (Rodrigues in 3-D via the internal
`_so3_exp`, the planar rotation in 2-D), and the translation is applied **directly**
— *not* through the $\mathfrak{se}(3)$ left-Jacobian. This is a deliberate
simplification: the chart is "axis-angle rotation $\oplus$ raw translation," which is
the screw decomposition only at the identity, but it is smooth, invertible near
identity, and — the point — **closed-form and GPU-native** (no cuSolver), so the
rigid optimiser's hot loop stays on-device. **DIFF-JAX**.

**`rigid_log(matrix, *, ndim)`** inverts it — recovering $(\omega, t)$ from a rigid
matrix. The axis-angle log (`_so3_log`) reads the angle off the trace
($\cos\theta=(\operatorname{tr}R-1)/2$, the standard rotation-trace identity) and the
axis off the antisymmetric part ($R-R^\top=2\sin\theta\,[\omega/\theta]_\times$), so
$\omega = \tfrac{\theta}{2\sin\theta}\,(r_{21}-r_{12},\,r_{02}-r_{20},\,r_{10}-r_{01})$.
It is valid for rotations **away from $\theta = \pi$** (the registration regime, where
per-iteration rotations are small); the antipodal case — where $\sin\theta\to0$ but the
axis is *not* recoverable from the antisymmetric part — is not handled.

Both `exp` and `log` carry a **small-angle guard**, and it is worth understanding
because it is a classic autodiff trap. Rodrigues divides by $\theta$ and
$\theta^2$; at $\omega=0$ those are $0/0$, and even though the *values* have finite
limits ($\sin\theta/\theta\to1$, $(1-\cos\theta)/\theta^2\to\tfrac12$), a naïve
implementation poisons the **gradient**. The trick `_so3_exp` uses: compute
$\theta^2=\|\omega\|^2$, and inside the small region replace it with a safe $1.0$
before taking the square root, then `jnp.where` in the Taylor approximations
($a\approx1-\theta^2/6$, $b\approx\tfrac12-\theta^2/24$). The reason the guard must be
on the value fed to `sqrt`, not just on the final `where`, is subtle:
$\mathrm{sqrt}'(0)=\infty$, and reverse-mode evaluates *both* branches of a `where`,
so the unselected branch's $0\cdot\infty=\text{NaN}$ leaks into the gradient even
though it is never "chosen." (Forward-mode is unaffected, so the bug hides until you
call `grad`.) This is the kind of detail the equation hides and the code must get
right.

### `affine_exp` and the general matrix exponential {#sec-affine-exp}

For the affine rung the constraint is weaker — we only need $\det>0$ (orientation
preserving and invertible), not orthogonality — and the algebra is correspondingly
larger: $\mathfrak{gl}(d)$, the space of **all** $d\times d$ matrices, is the tangent
space at the identity of $GL^+(d)$. There is no closed-form $\exp$ for a general
generator, so **`affine_exp(params, *, ndim)`** takes the first $d^2$ parameters as a
row-major generator matrix $A$, the last $d$ as the translation, and computes the
linear block as the **true matrix exponential** $\exp(A)$. Because $\det(e^A)=e^{\mathrm{tr}\,A}>0$
for any real $A$, *every* parameter vector maps to an invertible,
orientation-preserving map — the affine analogue of Rodrigues always returning a
proper rotation. The search space is unconstrained $\mathbb R^{d^2+d}$, and the chart
guarantees validity. **DIFF-JAX**.

The engine is **`matrix_exp`** in `linalg.matrix_function`, and its implementation is
chosen for the same GPU reason as `safe_*`: it is **scaling-and-squaring with a
truncated Taylor series**, *pure matmul*, no factorisation.

$$
e^A=\bigl(e^{A/2^s}\bigr)^{2^s},
\qquad
e^{A/2^s}\approx\sum_{k=0}^{q}\frac{(A/2^s)^k}{k!}.
$$ {#eq-scaling-squaring}

Scale $A$ down by $2^{-s}$ until its norm is tiny, sum a short Taylor series (the
small argument makes it converge fast), then **square the result $s$ times** to undo
the scaling — each squaring doubling the effective argument. With the defaults
($s=8$, order $12$) the scaled norm is negligible for any generator a registration
produces, so the result matches a reference `expm` to machine precision; the
alternative, `jax.scipy.linalg.expm` (Padé), needs a linear solve and so wedges or
falls back to CPU on the broken-cuSolver stacks. Pure matmul keeps `affine_exp`
`jit`- and `grad`-clean and on-device.

**`matrix_log`** is the inverse — **inverse scaling-and-squaring**: take $s$ matrix
square roots (by the **Denman–Beavers** iteration $Y_{k+1}=\tfrac12(Y_k+Z_k^{-1})$,
$Z_{k+1}=\tfrac12(Z_k+Y_k^{-1})$, which drives $Y_k\to M^{1/2}$ quadratically) to
push $A$ toward the identity, sum the Taylor series of $\log(I+X)$ on the small
remainder $X=A^{1/2^s}-I$, then scale back by $2^s$. The square-root step routes its
inverses through `safe_inv`, so unlike `matrix_exp` this is **not** for an optimiser
hot loop — it backs the offline Karcher mean of @sec-transform-manifold.

`matrix_log` carries one **domain caveat** you must respect: the principal logarithm
does not exist for a matrix with an eigenvalue on the **closed negative real axis** —
a reflection ($\det<0$) or a $180°$ rotation (eigenvalue $-1$). Rather than return
finite garbage there, `matrix_log` **returns NaN**: it checks the round-trip residual
$\|\exp(\log A)-A\|/\|A\|$ (itself pure matmul, cuSolver-free) against a tolerance and
NaNs out if the reconstruction fails. So a NaN means *"raise `n_sqrt` (the input may
merely exceed the Taylor radius); if still NaN, the input is genuinely out of
domain."* A loud failure, by design — the negative-real-axis boundary is also why a
near-antipodal cohort breaks the Karcher mean (@sec-transform-manifold).

### The `TransformModel`: `Rigid` and `Affine` {#sec-model}

The registration engine does not call `rigid_exp` / `affine_exp` directly; it holds a
**`TransformModel`** — a tiny frozen record that bundles the exponential chart with
the two pieces of parameter-layout knowledge the coarse-to-fine driver needs. The
`TransformModel` is a structural `Protocol` with immutable
`@dataclass(frozen=True)` implementers (`Rigid`, `Affine`), hashable so they ride as
`jit` static arguments. It exposes three methods:

- **`n_params(ndim)`** — the length of the parameter vector. `Rigid`: $1{+}d$ in 2-D,
  $3{+}d$ in 3-D (rotation DOF + translation). `Affine`: $d^2+d$. **HOST-QA** (a
  Python `int`).
- **`exp(params, *, ndim)`** — the chart, dispatching to `rigid_exp` or `affine_exp`.
  **DIFF-JAX**.
- **`rescale_to_grid(params, ratio)`** — when the coarse-to-fine driver warm-starts a
  finer pyramid level, the **translation** parameters (in voxel units) must scale by
  the per-axis `finer_shape / coarser_shape` ratio, while the rotation/linear block
  (dimensionless) stays put. Both models share `_rescale_translation_last`, which
  scales only the trailing `len(ratio)` parameters — the shared "linear block first,
  translation last" layout. **DIFF-JAX**.

Holding the layout *on the model* — rather than the driver slicing `params[-ndim:]` —
is what lets a new parametrisation (a 7-DOF similarity, a constrained rigid, the
rigid block a BBR recipe optimises) drop in as one record without touching the
driver. Note the dense-velocity-field family (diffeomorphic/SyN, the
[next rung](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf)) is **not** a `TransformModel`: its parameter is
a field, not a small vector, and it lowers onto a different driver.

## Closed-form affine from matched points {#sec-fit}

Optimisation is the general tool, but sometimes you already have **correspondences**
— matched landmark pairs, or the centroids of matched labels — and an affine between
two point sets has a **closed form**. No iteration, no Lie chart — one least-squares
solve.

**`fit_affine(source, target, weights=None)`** finds the affine $\mathbf A$ that maps
`target` onto `source` in the least-squares sense, minimising the (optionally
weighted) squared residual over the $M$ corresponding points. Write each point in
homogeneous form (append a $1$), stack them into $X$ (the homogeneous `target`,
$M\times(N{+}1)$) and $y$ (the `source`, $M\times N$). We want $\beta$ (the
transpose of $\mathbf A$) with $X\beta\approx y$. The least-squares solution is the
**normal equations**

$$
(X^\top W X)\,\beta = X^\top W\,y,
$$ {#eq-normal}

with $W$ the diagonal of `weights`. The Gram matrix $X^\top W X$ is **symmetric
positive-definite** (for non-degenerate, full-rank correspondences), so `fit_affine`
solves it by an **SPD Cholesky solve** (`safe_cho_solve`) — the cuSolver-robust seam
again — and transposes $\beta$ back into the $(N,N{+}1)$ rectangular affine. **DIFF-JAX**
(through `safe_*`), batched and N-D (unlike the rotation chart, which is 2-/3-D only).

Two practical notes. The weights let you **downweight outlier correspondences** (a
mismatched landmark gets a small $w$), the seed of robust fitting. And `fit_affine`
is the natural **closed-form initialiser** for the gradient-based optimiser: solve
the affine from a coarse correspondence first, then refine on the Lie chart. The two
parametrisations of this page — geometric factors (@sec-decomposition) and the
exponential chart (@sec-lie-exp) — meet here: you can `affine_matrix_to_params` a
`fit_affine` result to *read* its rotation and scale, or feed it as the warm start an
optimiser refines.

## Centering: rotate about the image centre {#sec-centering}

We have transforms and a way to fit them; the last piece of plumbing is **how a
matrix becomes sample coordinates**, and it hides a decision that quietly governs
whether the optimisation even works.

**`apply_affine(coords, matrix, *, center=None)`** applies a homogeneous matrix to a
field of coordinates, but with a twist: an optional **centre** $c$ about which the
linear part acts.

$$
\text{out}=M\,(p-c)+t+c.
$$ {#eq-apply}

With $c=0$ this is the plain $Mp+t$ of @eq-homogeneous. With $c\ne0$ it **subtracts
the centre, applies the linear map, then adds the centre back** — so the rotation and
scale pivot about $c$ rather than the coordinate origin. **`affine_grid(matrix,
spatial_shape, *, center=None)`** builds the absolute sample-coordinate grid the
resampler consumes: it lays down the `identity_grid` (index space, `grid[i] == i`,
`indexing='ij'`) and applies the matrix about the centre. When `center=None`,
`affine_grid` **defaults the centre to the grid centre** $(\text{shape}-1)/2$ — the
**registration convention** (rotate about the image centre, not the voxel at the
corner). Both are **DIFF-JAX**.

Why does this matter so much? Consider a tiny rotation of a $200^3$ image. About the
**origin** (the corner voxel), a $1°$ rotation moves the far corner by $200\cdot
\sin(1°)\approx 3.5$ voxels — the *translation* and *rotation* parameters are wildly
coupled, and a unit step in the rotation parameter moves the image enormously and
unevenly. The **rotation has huge leverage about the corner.** About the
**centre**, the same $1°$ rotation moves the farthest voxel by only $100\cdot\sin(1°)
\approx 1.75$ voxels, symmetrically, and rotation and translation decouple. In the
language of optimisation, centering **conditions the problem**: it equalises the
scales of the parameters so the Hessian is better-balanced, the Gauss–Newton step is
well-scaled, and the optimiser converges in far fewer iterations. The same logic is
why `fit_affine` is often run on **mean-centred** point sets. Centering is not
cosmetic; it is the difference between a problem that converges and one that crawls.

## Transforms form a manifold {#sec-transform-manifold}

Return to the motivating question with a twist: you have realigned every volume of an
fMRI run and now hold *one rigid transform per volume*. You want the **mean pose** of
the run (to define a reference), or you are building a study **template** and need the
average of a cohort of subject-to-MNI affines. The naïve move — average the matrices
entrywise — is **wrong**, and seeing why is the whole point of this section.

The set of rigid (or affine) transforms is not a vector space; it is a **curved
group** — a Riemannian manifold. The entrywise average of two rotations is *not a
rotation* (it is not orthogonal), and the entrywise average of two affines can be
**singular** even when both inputs are perfectly invertible. @fig-transforms-geodesic
makes this visible: interpolating between two affines by entrywise linear blend
collapses the figure through a near-degenerate state, while the geodesic stays a
clean rotation+scale throughout.

```{python}
#| label: fig-transforms-geodesic
#| fig-cap: "Why averaging on the manifold matters. Interpolating from the identity to an affine $B$ (a $176°$ rotation with a $1.4\\times$ scale). Left: the geodesic $\\exp(t\\,\\log B)$ — every intermediate is a clean rotation+scale, its determinant rising smoothly $1.0\\to1.96$ ($=s^2$). Right: the naive entrywise blend $(1-t)I+tB$ — at the midpoint the determinant collapses to $\\sim0.04$ (a near-singular, nearly-flattened square): the average of two well-conditioned transforms is degenerate. `transform_geodesic` / `transform_mean` walk the left path; an entrywise mean walks the right."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.linalg import det

# Minimal real 2x2 matrix exp / log (scaling-and-squaring + Denman-Beavers),
# the numpy analogue of nitrix's matrix_exp / matrix_log.
def m_exp(A, s=12, order=18):
    B = A / (2.0 ** s)
    term = np.eye(A.shape[0]); res = np.eye(A.shape[0])
    for k in range(1, order + 1):
        term = term @ B / k
        res = res + term
    for _ in range(s):
        res = res @ res
    return res

def m_sqrt(M, iters=40):
    Y, Z = M.copy(), np.eye(M.shape[0])
    for _ in range(iters):
        Y, Z = 0.5 * (Y + np.linalg.inv(Z)), 0.5 * (Z + np.linalg.inv(Y))
    return Y

def m_log(A, s=12, order=18):
    R = A.copy()
    for _ in range(s):
        R = m_sqrt(R)
    X = R - np.eye(A.shape[0])
    Xk = X.copy(); ser = X.copy()
    for k in range(2, order + 1):
        Xk = Xk @ X
        ser = ser + ((-1.0) ** (k + 1)) * Xk / k
    return ser * (2.0 ** s)

def hom(M2, t):
    H = np.eye(3); H[:2, :2] = M2; H[:2, 2] = t; return H

A = np.eye(3)
ang = np.radians(176.0)
Rb = np.array([[np.cos(ang), -np.sin(ang)], [np.sin(ang), np.cos(ang)]])
B = hom(1.4 * Rb, [0.6, -0.3])

ts = np.linspace(0, 1, 7)
logB = m_log(B)
geo = [m_exp(t * logB) for t in ts]
lerp = [(1 - t) * A + t * B for t in ts]

sq = np.array([[-0.35, 0.35, 0.35, -0.35, -0.35],
               [-0.35, -0.35, 0.35, 0.35, -0.35]])
def apply(H, pts):
    p = np.vstack([pts, np.ones((1, pts.shape[1]))]); return (H @ p)[:2]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5.2))
cmap = plt.cm.plasma
for ax, fams, title in [
    (axes[0], geo, "Geodesic  $\\exp(t\\,\\log B)$ — stays a rotation+scale"),
    (axes[1], lerp, "Naive entrywise lerp  $(1-t)I + tB$ — collapses"),
]:
    for j, (t, H) in enumerate(zip(ts, fams)):
        out = apply(H, sq)
        col = cmap(j / (len(ts) - 1))
        ax.plot(out[0], out[1], color=col, lw=1.8,
                label=f"$t={t:.2f}$  det$={det(H[:2,:2]):+.2f}$")
    ax.set_aspect('equal'); ax.set_xlim(-2.2, 2.2); ax.set_ylim(-2.2, 2.2)
    ax.axhline(0, color='0.9', lw=0.5); ax.axvline(0, color='0.9', lw=0.5)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.legend(fontsize=6.5, loc='upper left', framealpha=0.85)

fig.suptitle("Averaging transforms must respect the manifold", fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.95])
plt.show()
```

The cure is to do everything **through the chart**: map to the flat algebra with
`log`, do the linear-algebra operation there, and map back with `exp`. `nitrix`
deliberately uses the **true matrix chart** (`matrix_log`/`matrix_exp`) here, not the
closed-form `rigid_log` — because the matrix log is smooth at the identity, whereas
the $\mathfrak{so}(n)$ rotation-vector log has a singularity the iteration's
identity-init would hit.

- **`transform_geodesic(transform, t)`** — the point at fraction $t$ along the
  geodesic from identity to `transform`, $\exp(t\,\log T)$. At $t=0$ it is the
  identity, at $t=1$ it is $T$, and at **$t=\tfrac12$ it is the matrix square root** —
  $\text{geodesic}(T,\tfrac12)^2=T$ exactly, which an entrywise half-blend can never
  guarantee. To interpolate between two transforms $A$ and $B$, compose:
  $A\cdot\text{geodesic}(A^{-1}B,t)$. Offline (uses `matrix_log`).

- **`transform_mean(transforms, *, weights=None, iters=10, return_residual=False)`** —
  the **Fréchet (Karcher) mean** of a stack of homogeneous transforms: the Riemannian
  barycentre, the point minimising the sum of squared geodesic distances. It is found
  by the standard log/exp **fixed point**

  $$
  \mu \;\leftarrow\; \mu\cdot\exp\!\Big(\sum_k w_k\,\log(\mu^{-1}T_k)\Big),
  $$ {#eq-karcher}

  starting from $\mu=T_0$: at each step, map every transform into the tangent space
  *at the current estimate* ($\log(\mu^{-1}T_k)$), take the ordinary weighted mean
  there (the algebra *is* flat), and step $\mu$ by the $\exp$ of that mean tangent.
  The mean of rigid transforms is rigid; of affines, affine. Two design choices to
  know. `iters` is a **hard cap, not a tolerance loop** — a data-dependent
  `float(residual) < tol` break would forfeit the `jit`/`grad`-cleanliness the op
  advertises, so a *widely dispersed* cohort (rotation spread $\gtrsim\pi/2$) may not
  converge within the default 10; raise `iters` and pass `return_residual=True` to
  *check* convergence (the residual $\|\sum_k w_k\log(\mu^{-1}T_k)\|\to0$ at a true
  barycentre) rather than trust it. And a **near-antipodal** cohort (a pair $\approx\pi$
  apart) sits on `matrix_log`'s negative-real-axis boundary (@sec-affine-exp): the
  tangent is NaN there and propagates to a NaN mean — a loud "the mean is ill-defined,"
  not silent garbage. Offline (`matrix_log` ⇒ `safe_inv`).

- **`velocity_mean(velocities, *, weights=None)`** — the Fréchet mean of **stationary
  velocity fields**. The beautiful simplification: an SVF *is* its own Lie-algebra
  element (it lives in the tangent space already; its $\exp$ is the diffeomorphism,
  per the [SVF page](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf)), so its barycentre is just the
  ordinary **(weighted) arithmetic mean** — no log/exp loop at all. This is the
  Lie-algebra property in its purest form, and it is **DIFF-JAX** and GPU-native
  (unlike `transform_mean`), the substrate for deformable-template centring.

- **`fuse_transforms(transforms, shape, *, mode='nearest')`** — collapse a multi-stage
  pipeline (homogeneous matrices *and* displacement fields, interleaved) into **one
  displacement field** on the output grid, so the moving image is resampled **once**.
  The stages are listed in application order (`transforms[0]` first, as a rigid →
  affine → deformable pipeline runs), and `fuse_transforms` composes them on the
  sampling grid in **reverse** — $g=T_1(T_2(\dots T_N(x)))$ — applying matrices about
  the grid centre (the @sec-centering convention) and adding sampled displacements,
  then subtracts the identity grid to return a relative displacement. The payoff is
  both **quality** (no compounded resampling blur — every intermediate interpolation
  is a quality loss) and **throughput** (one gather instead of $N$). It uses no
  `safe_inv`, so it is GPU-native.

The use case ties the page together. To build a study template you align each subject
to a working reference, take the `transform_mean` of the subject-to-reference affines
to define the *average pose*, and iterate — the groupwise-registration loop, with the
barycentre as its centring step. To summarise head motion across runs you take the
mean (or the geodesic spread) of the per-volume rigid transforms. And to apply a
rigid + affine + warp pipeline you `fuse_transforms` it into a single field and
resample once. Every one of these respects the manifold; none of them would be
correct as an entrywise average.

## Further reading

- Hartley, R. and Zisserman, A. (2004). *Multiple View Geometry in Computer Vision*,
  2nd ed. Cambridge University Press. — Homogeneous coordinates and the affine/
  projective transform groups, from first principles.
- Murray, R. M., Li, Z., and Sastry, S. S. (1994). *A Mathematical Introduction to
  Robotic Manipulation*. CRC Press. — The classic, readable account of $SE(3)$,
  twists, and the exponential map (Rodrigues, screw motions).
- Hall, B. C. (2015). *Lie Groups, Lie Algebras, and Representations*, 2nd ed.
  Springer. — The matrix-Lie-group exponential/logarithm and the BCH formula, at the
  right level of rigour.
- Higham, N. J. (2008). *Functions of Matrices: Theory and Computation*. SIAM. —
  Scaling-and-squaring for `expm`, inverse scaling-and-squaring and the
  Denman–Beavers iteration for `logm`/`sqrtm`, and their conditioning.
- Pennec, X., Fillard, P., and Ayache, N. (2006). "A Riemannian Framework for Tensor
  Computing." *International Journal of Computer Vision*, 66(1), 41–66. — The Fréchet
  mean and geodesic interpolation on a matrix manifold; the template-construction
  rationale.
- Arsigny, V., Commowick, O., Pennec, X., and Ayache, N. (2006). "A Log-Euclidean
  Framework for Statistics on Diffeomorphisms." *MICCAI 2006*. — Why averaging
  transforms goes through the log domain; the SVF-as-algebra view.
- Cox, R. W. and Jesmanowicz, A. (1999). "Real-Time 3D Image Registration for
  Functional MRI." *Magnetic Resonance in Medicine*, 42(6), 1014–1018. — The
  `3dvolreg` lineage: rigid realignment of an fMRI series as iterated linearised
  least squares.

## See also

- The exponential charts and grid application —
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/transform.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/transform.py).
- The geometric $T R S E$ factorisation, fit, compose/invert —
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/affine.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/affine.py).
- The manifold barycentre / geodesic / fusion —
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/algebra.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/algebra.py).
- The general matrix `exp` / `log` —
  [`src/nitrix/linalg/matrix_function.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/matrix_function.py).
- The cuSolver-robust solver seam —
  [`src/nitrix/linalg/_solver.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_solver.py).
- The `Rigid` / `Affine` transform models —
  [`src/nitrix/register/_model.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_model.py).
- Neighbouring pages: the transform ladder and pull-back framing
  ([Foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-ladder)); how a sample grid becomes a warped
  image ([Resampling](resampling.qmd#sec-pullback-sampling)); the optimiser that
  moves on the Lie chart ([Optimization](optimization.qmd#sec-gauss-newton)); and the
  next rung, dense velocity fields ([Diffeomorphic](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf)).
